directions.name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
directions.hypothesis: Round 7/100 proved that the bounded PTX export-address cleanup is still live on top of the restored grouped-rows-4 PTX surface: runtime improved from 24.180656 ms to a new best custom 24.164352 ms with correctness intact. But the gain was only 0.016304 ms, small enough that the search policy still tagged it PASS_FLAT and left the accepted base unchanged. The counters also say the export family is nearing saturation rather than exposing a fresh win path: barrier only nudged down from 5.48 to 5.42, long-scoreboard ticked up from 7.17 to 7.26, and active warps stayed pinned at 16.62. That makes the still-open PTX control-path family the best next move, because it keeps the restored surface and the cleaned export helper but attacks the remaining inner-loop control / consume-order overhead that the narrow export cleanup could not fully remove.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX hot-band control-path overhead and scoreboard / barrier balance on the restored grouped-rows-4 PTX surface, not another export-address-only cleanup.
directions.risk: Moderate. This family is still live and remains the top frontier representative, but another too-small control-order nudge can easily collapse back into sub-threshold noise on an already good PTX base.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164352 ms new-best control run
ranking_notes: The export-address family just delivered a real but sub-threshold gain, which is exactly the signal to rotate back to the highest-ranked still-open adjacent family rather than dig deeper into the same cleanup branch.
This stays on the restored PTX winner and reuses the export cleanup as a better base, so it offers more upside than another bounded export-only tweak.
It is ranked ahead of the barrier-retime family because it is both higher on the live frontier and broader than a pure handoff-only adjustment.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if another bounded PTX control-path adjustment again lands as PASS_FLAT noise or if it worsens both scoreboard and barrier while leaving active warps pinned.

directions.name: Steady-state Barrier / Handoff Retime
directions.hypothesis: The latest run suggests the export cleanup harvested most of the easy writeback win but did not convert it into an accepted-base transition. Barrier did improve slightly, from 5.48 to 5.42, while long-scoreboard moved the wrong way and occupancy stayed fixed. That combination is consistent with the active PTX branch now being exposed at the steady-state wait-group / handoff boundary rather than at the final export address surface itself. A very narrow retime around the PTX hot-band `cp.async` wait, `__syncthreads()`, and future-tile refill sequence is therefore the best second family: it stays on the same restored PTX branch, targets the most visible surviving scheduler friction directly, and avoids reopening the deeper export variants that already looked fragile historically.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier latency and stage-handoff cadence in the active PTX hot-band steady-state loop, not grouped-row mapping or epilogue bookkeeping.
directions.risk: Moderate. A handoff retime is still bounded, but it can easily trade one exposed stall class for another if the wait/commit ordering lengthens the active PTX critical path.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164352 ms run
ranking_notes: Barrier is the one headline stall that actually improved under the export cleanup, which makes a direct handoff retime the cleanest way to continue exploiting that signal without repeating the same family.
This remains on the current PTX winner, unlike the off-branch fallback families, so it can still convert a small flat gain into a meaningful accepted-base move.
It stays below the broader PTX control-path family because the handoff-only version is more bounded and therefore lower-upside.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the handoff retime leaves barrier effectively unchanged or lowers barrier only by raising long-scoreboard enough to keep runtime flat.

directions.name: Trim The Grouped-Row 128x128 Sibling Export Scratch To The PTX-Style Single Stage
directions.hypothesis: Round-history rehydration should keep at least one credible off-branch family alive while the loop continues exploiting the restored PTX surface. The strongest such escape hatch is still the grouped-row 128x128 sibling export-scratch trim, which historically measured 24.422464 ms and remains a clean alternative if the active PTX-local families now flatten out. It stays third because the restored PTX branch just produced the latest win and should still be exploited first, but this sibling export family is the best preserved fallback that attacks the same general export-lifetime theme without being trapped inside the current PTX control surface.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Export scratch lifetime and writeback overhead on the grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling path, used as an off-branch fallback if the active PTX path stalls.
directions.risk: Low to medium. The family has historical evidence and bounded scope, but taking it now means leaving the PTX branch that just established the latest best custom run.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164352 ms new-best PTX run
ranking_notes: This keeps one non-PTX export family alive after the round-history queue reinjection instead of letting the frontier collapse onto only two PTX-local loops.
It is ranked below the two active-branch PTX families because the latest run was still a real new best custom and should be exploited before rotating away.
If both PTX-local follow-ups behave like PASS_FLAT again, this branch becomes the cleanest next escape hatch.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the sibling export-scratch trim cannot get close to its own 24.422464 ms historical band or if leaving the restored PTX path immediately gives the small new-best gain back.

notes: Round 8/100 audit: the bounded PTX export-address cleanup turned the restored grouped-rows-4 PTX surface into a new best custom run at 24.164352 ms, but only by 0.016304 ms, so the current search policy still labeled the transition PASS_FLAT and did not promote the accepted base. The metric movement also says the family is nearing saturation rather than opening a fresh dominant win path: barrier improved slightly from 5.48 to 5.42, long-scoreboard ticked up from 7.17 to 7.26, and active warps stayed pinned at 16.62. The ranking therefore rotates the recommendation back to the still-open PTX control-path family, keeps one direct barrier / handoff retime alive on the same restored surface, and preserves one off-branch grouped-row export family as the fallback maintained by the round-history live-queue rehydration.
